purpose: Central Redis engine hosting all 15 SONiC databases for inter-container communication. Every other container depends on this one being healthy. Provides the shared state bus (pub/sub + keyspace notifications) that drives the entire SONiC config-to-ASIC pipeline. Without this container, no other SONiC service can start or operate.

daemons.name: redis-server
daemons.role: Single Redis instance hosting all 15 SONiC logical databases (DB 0-14) on a UNIX socket (/var/run/redis/redis.sock) and TCP port 6379. Each logical DB is dedicated to a specific data plane: CONFIG_DB (DB4) holds persistent switch configuration, APPL_DB (DB0) is the application-level state bus, ASIC_DB (DB1) holds SAI object state, STATE_DB (DB6) holds operational status, COUNTERS_DB (DB2) holds interface/ACL counters. Uses RDB persistence for CONFIG_DB durability. Pub/sub keyspace notifications on all DBs drive the *syncd/*mgrd daemon pipeline.

reads: CONFIG_DB (DB4): all tables on startup from /etc/sonic/config_db.json RDB snapshot
writes: All 15 DBs: accepts writes from all containers; persists CONFIG_DB to /etc/sonic/config_db.json via redis-dump or config save

daemons.name: redis-sentinel
daemons.role: Optional Redis Sentinel process used only in multi-ASIC chassis deployments. Monitors redis-server health, triggers failover if the primary Redis instance becomes unavailable. Not present in single-ASIC deployments. When active, listens on port 26379 and coordinates with remote chassis supervisor processes.

writes: Sentinel-internal metadata; does not write application DBs directly

key_data_flows.path: config load /etc/sonic/config_db.json -> sonic-cfggen parses JSON -> redis-cli writes to CONFIG_DB (DB4) -> keyspace notifications published -> all *mgrd daemons in swss/bgp/lldp/etc receive notifications and apply config
key_data_flows.notes: This is the authoritative config bootstrap path. If CONFIG_DB is empty or corrupt after reboot, all containers start but apply no configuration -- switch behaves as unconfigured.

key_data_flows.path: Any producer (fpmsyncd, portmgrd, vlanmgrd, etc.) writes to APPL_DB (DB0) -> Redis keyspace notification published on DB0 -> orchagent ConsumerStateTable wakes up -> orchagent processes entry -> writes SAI objects to ASIC_DB (DB1) -> syncd notified
key_data_flows.notes: The entire config-to-ASIC pipeline is event-driven via Redis pub/sub. A slow or backlogged Redis causes all producers to block on publish acknowledgement, stalling the pipeline.

key_data_flows.path: User runs 'config save' -> sonic-cfggen reads CONFIG_DB (DB4) via redis-cli -> serializes to JSON -> writes /etc/sonic/config_db.json -> survives reboot
key_data_flows.notes: Changes written directly to CONFIG_DB via redis-cli or config commands are live immediately but NOT persistent until 'config save' is run. Reboot without save discards changes.

key_data_flows.path: syncd flex-counter thread reads ASIC counters via SAI -> writes to COUNTERS_DB (DB2) at configured polling interval -> SNMP agent / CLI (show interfaces counters) reads COUNTERS_DB
key_data_flows.notes: Counter DB is write-heavy. On high port-count platforms, counter data can accumulate rapidly. COUNTERS_DB is not persisted across reboots -- all counters reset to zero on restart.

warm_restart.mechanism: Redis server does not participate in SONiC warm restart. The database container must remain running continuously during warm restart of other containers. If the database container itself restarts, all in-memory DB state (APPL_DB, ASIC_DB, STATE_DB, etc.) is lost and all dependent containers must perform a cold restart to repopulate state.
warm_restart.enable_command: N/A -- database container warm restart not supported
warm_restart.caveat: Never restart the database container during a network warm restart window. Doing so will cause all containers to lose their DB connection and trigger full cold re-initialization.

restart_impact: Catastrophic -- restarting the database container drops all Redis connections simultaneously. Every running SONiC container (swss, syncd, bgp, teamd, lldp, etc.) loses its DB handle and will either crash or enter a reconnect loop. APPL_DB, ASIC_DB, and STATE_DB are fully cleared (non-persistent). The ASIC continues forwarding with its last-programmed state until syncd reconnects and reconciles. Full recovery requires all dependent containers to restart in dependency order, which systemd/supervisord handles automatically but takes 30-120 seconds on typical platforms.

restart_commands.redis_only: docker exec database supervisorctl restart redis

state_verification_commands: redis-cli -p 6379 info keyspace (show all DBs with key counts)
redis-cli -n 4 dbsize (CONFIG_DB key count -- should be non-zero after config load)
redis-cli -p 6379 client list | wc -l (connected client count)

symptoms: redis-cli -p 6379 info memory shows used_memory approaching maxmemory
OOM killer terminates redis-server process (dmesg shows 'redis-server killed')
redis-cli commands return 'OOM command not allowed when used memory > maxmemory'
COUNTERS_DB or ASIC_DB writes start failing -- new counter keys rejected
orchagent or syncd log shows Redis ENOMEM errors on write attempts
causes: Large routing table (100k+ routes) causing ASIC_DB and APPL_DB to grow proportionally
Counter accumulation in COUNTERS_DB -- high port-count platform with many ACL/flow entries
Memory leak in a producer daemon writing duplicate or unreferenced keys
FLEX_COUNTER_DB accumulation from high polling frequency on many objects
maxmemory not set (default unlimited) but system RAM exhausted by all SONiC processes combined
diagnosis: redis-cli -p 6379 info memory | grep -E 'used_memory_human|maxmemory_human|mem_fragmentation_ratio'
redis-cli -p 6379 info keyspace -- identify which DB has unexpectedly large key count
redis-cli -n 2 dbsize -- COUNTERS_DB; redis-cli -n 1 dbsize -- ASIC_DB
redis-cli -n 1 keys '*' | awk -F: '{print $2}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -20 -- find top object types in ASIC_DB
dmesg | grep -i 'oom\|killed\|redis' -- check for OOM kills
show processes memory -- check total system memory consumption
resolution: If COUNTERS_DB bloated: reduce flex-counter polling frequency in CONFIG_DB FLEX_COUNTER_TABLE or disable unused counter groups
If ASIC_DB / APPL_DB growing due to route scale: reduce BGP table size, use 'maximum-paths' or route filtering in FRR
Set Redis maxmemory-policy to 'noeviction' (default) and set maxmemory to 75% of available RAM in redis.conf to trigger explicit errors rather than silent OOM
Flush stale counters: redis-cli -n 2 FLUSHDB (WARNING: resets all counters to zero; non-persistent so safe)
If OOM-killed: add swap space temporarily to survive until table scale is reduced

common_issues.issue: CONFIG_DB persistence failure -- changes lost on reboot
symptoms: Switch reboots and comes up with default/empty configuration
Interfaces and routing config absent after reboot
'config show' or 'show running-configuration' reflects previous session changes but 'show startup-configuration' does not
/etc/sonic/config_db.json timestamp is old (predates recent config changes)
'config save' command returns error or hangs
causes: Operator made config changes via CLI but did not run 'config save' before reboot
Filesystem /etc/sonic is read-only due to disk fault or out-of-space condition
sonic-cfggen fails during config save due to corrupt CONFIG_DB state (unexpected key format)
Redis RDB persistence disabled and operator assumed changes were auto-saved
config save process killed mid-write, leaving corrupt config_db.json
diagnosis: diff <(redis-cli -n 4 --no-auth-warning keys '*' | sort) <(python3 -c "import json; print('\n'.join(json.load(open('/etc/sonic/config_db.json')).keys()))" | sort) -- compare live DB vs saved file
ls -la /etc/sonic/config_db.json -- check modification timestamp
df -h /etc/sonic -- check for full filesystem
config save 2>&1 -- attempt save and capture error output
cat /etc/sonic/config_db.json | python3 -m json.tool > /dev/null -- validate JSON syntax of saved file
resolution: Always run 'config save' after making configuration changes: sudo config save -y
If filesystem full: identify and remove large files (find /var/core -name '*.core' -size +100M), then retry config save
If config_db.json corrupt: restore from backup at /etc/sonic/config_db.json.bak (if exists) or reconstruct from golden config
To auto-save on change: consider adding a post-change hook or periodic cron calling 'config save'
Verify persistence after save: python3 -m json.tool /etc/sonic/config_db.json | grep -c '"' -- should return non-zero

common_issues.issue: Redis pub/sub backlog -- daemons miss or delay processing updates
symptoms: Config changes applied via CLI but take abnormally long to take effect (minutes instead of seconds)
redis-cli -p 6379 info stats shows growing 'blocked_clients' count
orchagent or mgrd logs show timestamps far behind wall clock on received notifications
redis-cli -p 6379 client list shows clients with 'cmd=subscribe' and large 'multi' values
Redis CPU usage high while application throughput is low
causes: A slow consumer daemon (e.g., orchagent under heavy SAI load) not draining its notification queue fast enough
Notification storm -- mass config push (loading full config_db.json) generates thousands of keyspace events simultaneously
Orchagent stuck in a retry loop on a failing SAI operation, blocking its main select() loop from processing new notifications
High counter polling frequency in FLEX_COUNTER_DB generating excessive pub/sub traffic on COUNTERS_DB channel
Redis client-output-buffer-limit exceeded causing client disconnection and reconnect storms
diagnosis: redis-cli -p 6379 info clients | grep -E 'blocked_clients|connected_clients'
redis-cli -p 6379 info stats | grep -E 'pubsub_channels|pubsub_patterns|total_commands_processed'
redis-cli -p 6379 client list | grep -v 'cmd=ping' -- identify slow/blocked clients by name and pending buffer size
redis-cli -p 6379 monitor (use briefly -- high overhead) -- observe notification rate
grep -E 'Select|timeout|retry' /var/log/swss/orchagent.log | tail -50 -- check orchagent select loop health
redis-cli -p 6379 info stats | grep 'client_recent_max_output_buffer'
resolution: Identify the slow consumer via 'client list' output; restart the specific daemon if it is stuck
If notification storm from bulk config load: spread config pushes across time or use config reload which batches writes
Reduce FLEX_COUNTER polling frequency: redis-cli -n 4 hset 'FLEX_COUNTER_TABLE|PORT_STAT_COUNTER' POLL_INTERVAL 10000 (10s instead of 1s default)
If orchagent retry loop: check orchagent.log for repeated 'SAI_STATUS' errors on same object; resolve underlying SAI issue
Increase client-output-buffer-limit in redis.conf if buffer overflow is root cause: client-output-buffer-limit pubsub 64mb 32mb 60

common_issues.issue: Database container startup failure -- Redis fails to bind, all dependent containers cannot start
symptoms: sudo systemctl status database shows 'failed' or 'activating' indefinitely
docker ps shows database container not running or in restart loop
All other containers (swss, syncd, bgp, teamd, etc.) fail to start with 'connection refused' to Redis
redis-cli -p 6379 ping returns 'Connection refused'
Journalctl shows: 'Creating Server TCP listening socket 127.0.0.1:6379: bind: Address already in use'
Full system comes up but 'show ip route' and 'show interfaces' return empty output
causes: Stale Redis process holding port 6379 from a previous unclean shutdown
Corrupt RDB dump file (/var/lib/redis/dump.rdb) preventing Redis from loading -- Redis exits on parse error
Insufficient file descriptor limit (ulimit -n) preventing Redis from opening enough socket connections
Another process (rogue redis-server or test process) bound to port 6379
UNIX socket file /var/run/redis/redis.sock left over from prior crash with wrong permissions
diagnosis: sudo systemctl status database -- check exit code and last error message
journalctl -u database -n 50 --no-pager -- detailed startup error sequence
docker logs database 2>&1 | tail -50 -- Redis startup output inside container
ss -tlnp | grep 6379 -- check if port is already in use
ls -la /var/run/redis/redis.sock -- check socket file existence and permissions
ls -la /var/lib/redis/dump.rdb -- check RDB file size and modification time
sudo -u redis redis-check-rdb /var/lib/redis/dump.rdb -- validate RDB file integrity
resolution: If stale process: kill $(lsof -t -i:6379) then sudo systemctl start database
If stale socket: rm /var/run/redis/redis.sock then sudo systemctl start database
If corrupt RDB: mv /var/lib/redis/dump.rdb /var/lib/redis/dump.rdb.corrupt && sudo systemctl start database (Redis starts empty -- you must reload config: config load /etc/sonic/config_db.json)
If file descriptor limit: verify /etc/security/limits.conf has 'redis soft nofile 65536' and 'redis hard nofile 65536'
After database container recovers: sudo systemctl restart swss syncd bgp teamd lldp -- force all dependent containers to reconnect

scale_considerations.key_count_limits: Redis itself has no hard key limit but memory is the practical constraint. A fully loaded spine router with 1M FIB entries will have ASIC_DB and APPL_DB holding 3-5M keys combined. Monitor 'redis-cli info keyspace' regularly on high-scale deployments.
scale_considerations.memory_sizing: Rule of thumb: allocate 512MB Redis maxmemory for moderate scale (100k routes, 1k neighbors), 2GB for high scale (1M routes, 50k+ ACL entries). Set maxmemory in /etc/redis/redis.conf and restart database container.
scale_considerations.counter_db_growth: COUNTERS_DB grows with port count * counter types * polling frequency. On a 128-port platform with 1-second polling, COUNTERS_DB can exceed 200MB. Set FLEX_COUNTER polling interval to 10s or higher for monitoring use cases.
scale_considerations.connection_count: Each daemon maintains 2-4 Redis connections (one per subscribed DB). A fully deployed SONiC switch has 50-100 Redis clients. Monitor 'connected_clients' in 'redis-cli info clients'.
scale_considerations.pub_sub_channels: Each keyspace-notified DB creates one channel per key on modification. High-churn tables (COUNTERS_DB, FLEX_COUNTER_DB) can create thousands of simultaneous pub/sub events. Reduce polling frequency if pubsub_channels count stays persistently high.
